revision.text:
[[ja:ヘボソ塚古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site | name = Heboso Tsuka Kofun | image = [[File:Heboso Tsuka Kofun Shutsudo Dōkyō.jpg|280px]]
Bronze mirrors excavated from Heboso Tsuka Kofun | alternate_name = Henposo Tsuka Kofun (扁保曽塚古墳) | location = {{ill|Kobe|qid=Q48320|1=Kobe}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.727963888889|N|135.274625|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) | built = Early [[Kofun period]] | area = Total length '''63 m''' | Historic Site1_notes = None | burial goods = 6 bronze mirrors, 2 stone bracelets, magatama beads, sugeta beads, small beads | architectural_styles= {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} }} '''Heboso Tsuka Kofun''' (へぼそづかこふん, also '''Henposo Tsuka Kofun''') was an '''early Kofun-period Kofun''' located in '''Okamoto 1-chome, Higashinada Ward, Kobe, Hyōgo Prefecture'''. It was constructed on a '''south-facing slope of the Rokko Mountains''', oriented '''northwest-west''', and had a total length of '''63 meters''', typeing a '''keyhole-shaped mound (zenpō-kōen-fun)'''. According to the *Settsu Meisho Zue* (*Illustrated Guide to the Famous Places of Settsu*, Edo period), '''about 20 smaller Kofuns''' were located nearby. However, after the land was '''privatized in 1897 (Meiji 30)''', it was completely destroyed due to '''urban development''', and the site later became the '''residence of Nagao Ryōkichi'''.[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1174477/1/79 神社と史蹟] Today, only a '''stone omitted inscribed with "Henposo Tsuka Site"''' remains. == burial goods == [[File:ヘボソ塚出土銅鏡他.jpg|thumb|275x275px|Bronze mirrors and other items excavated from Heboso Tsuka Kofun (*Motoyama Village Chronicle*)]] [[File:ヘボソ塚古墳出土 三角縁三神二獣鏡.JPG|thumb|Sankakuen Sanshin Nijūkyō (*Tokyo National Museum*)]] An excavation in '''1895 (Meiji 28)''' uncovered '''grave goods''' from the '''vertical stone chamber (tateana-shiki sekishitsu)''' in the mound’s circular rear section. These included: *'''6 bronze mirrors''', including '''two Sankakuen-shinju-kyō''' (*triangular-rimmed divine-beast mirrors*). *'''2 stone bracelets (ishikazura)''' *'''Various beads''', including '''magatama''', '''sugeta beads''', and '''small beads'''. These burial goods are now housed in the '''Tokyo National Museum'''.{{cite encyclopedia | editor = Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center | encyclopedia = Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia | title = Heboso Tsuka Kofun | year = 1983 | isbn = 4-87521-100-7 | pages = 925 (Volume 2) }} Among the mirrors: *'''Two are triangular-rimmed divine-beast mirrors (sankakuen-shinju-kyō)''', which are of '''the same casting mold''' (*dōhan-kyō*) as those found in: ''''''Higashi-Kurumazuka Kofun''' (*part of the {{ill|Himeyama Kofun Cluster|ja|日岡山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himeyama Kofun Cluster|1=Hiokayama Kofun Group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q98492835}} in Kakogawa, Hyōgo Prefecture*). ''''''{{ill|Chōhōji Minamihara Kofun|ja|長法寺南原古墳|1=Chōhōji Minamihara Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōhōji Minamihara Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōhōji Minamihara Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q123116949}}''' (*Nagaokakyō, Kyoto Prefecture*), which has '''7 other mirrors from the same mold'''. == Etymology and Legends == The name '''"Heboso"''' (*ヘボソ*) first appears in historical records in the '''16th century''', but '''its origin remains unknown'''.{{Cite book|和書 |editor=[[Michitani Takashi (道谷卓)]] |date=1989 |title=Japan's History in Higashinada |publisher=Kobe City Cultural Promotion Foundation, Kobe City Higashinada Cultural Center |pages=36-37 }} === Association with Ariwara no Narihira === A '''local legend''' once identified the mound as the '''tomb of Ariwara no Narihira''', a famous Heian-era courtier and poet. This was mentioned in the *Settsu-shi* (Records of Settsu Province). However, modern scholars '''dismiss this theory''', as the Kofun '''predates Narihira’s lifetime'''. === Connection to Textile Folklore === A '''folk song''' from the area mentions the mound: *"At Osaba in Okamoto stands Heboso Tsuka, where cloth-weaving people dwell."* The *Settsu Meisho Zue* also records this '''but notes that its meaning was already lost''' by the Edo period. However, '''a legend persists''' that on the '''second day of the lunar new year''', one can hear the '''sound of a weaver’s loom from the mound'''.{{Cite book|和書 |author = [[Tanabe Makoto (田辺眞人)]] |year = 1998 |title = The Legends of Kobe (Revised Edition) |publisher = Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center |isbn = 4-87521-076-0 |pages =120 }} Michitani Takashi (道谷卓) suggests that the term '''"Osaba"''' (*をさば*) in the folk song is derived from '''"Osa" (筬)''', referring to '''the reed used in weaving'''. This supports the idea that the '''legend and folk song may originate from an association with textile artisans'''. == References == {{Reflist}} ## Categories [[zh-yue:神戸市]] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|ヘボソ塚古墳|version=99534787|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Heboso Kofun]] [[zh:三角縁神獣鏡が確認された主な古墳]] [[ja:ヘボソ塚古墳]] [[ja:東求女塚古墳]] [[ja:コヤダニ古墳]] [[ja:洲本市]] [[ja:吉島古墳]] [[ja:たつの市]] [[ja:森尾古墳]] [[ja:豊岡市]] [[ja:備前車塚古墳]] [[ja:岡山市]] [[ja:鶴山丸山古墳]] [[ja:備前市]] [[ja:白鳥古墳]] [[ja:東広島市]] [[ja:潮崎山古墳]] [[ja:福山市]] [[ja:中小田1号墳]] [[ja:広島市]] [[ja:神原神社古墳]] [[ja:雲南市]] [[ja:竹島御家老屋敷古墳]] [[ja:周南市]] [[ja:奥三号墳]] [[ja:さぬき市]] [[ja:西山古墳]] [[ja:宮谷古墳]] [[ja:徳島市]] [[ja:那珂八幡古墳]] [[ja:福岡市]] [[ja:石塚山古墳]] [[ja:京都郡]] [[ja:一貴山銚子塚古墳]] [[ja:糸島市]] [[ja:岩上祭祀遺構]] [[ja:宗像市]] [[ja:赤塚古墳]] [[ja:宇佐市]] [[ja:川部・高森古墳群]] [[ja:持田古墳群]] [[ja:児湯郡]] [[ja:高鍋町]] {{wikidata link|Q11336594}} [[Category:Archaeological sites in Japan]] [[Category:History of Higashinada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Kofun of Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]
